analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Wes Schultz -- Style & Profile

- Schultz is primarily a grappler who wins by strangling opponents out — two of his last four fights ended in submissions, and his submission rate of 1.1 attempts per 15 minutes reflects a fighter who actively hunts finishes on the ground rather than grinding for top control.
- His striking is a delivery mechanism, not a weapon. Career accuracy sits at 43%, and the 4.97 significant strikes absorbed per minute is a genuine red flag — he walks into damage at a higher rate than he dishes it out.
- He has been knocked out twice in his last four fights, both within the first two rounds (R1 against Pinas, R2 against Abdul-Malik). That pattern — early stoppage losses under striking pressure — flags a real vulnerability against anyone who can put him in danger on the feet before the fight hits the mat.
- When the grappling works, it works fast. His submission win over Mingaj came in under two minutes, and even his win over Johnston here went to Round 3 — suggesting he can be patient when needed, but prefers to finish quickly once he gets the fight where he wants it.
- At 29, he's on the right side of the age curve, and his 6'1" frame with a 77-inch reach gives him a modest physical edge over Johnston.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Ben Johnston -- Style & Profile

- Johnston is making his UFC debut here — at 35 years old, that's a late arrival to the promotion, and every number in his UFC statistical profile reads zero because this fight was his first and only UFC appearance.
- His only UFC fight is the rematch with Schultz on this very card, which means the entire analytical picture has to be built from that single data point: he lost by submission in Round 3, landing strikes at a rate of 0.59 per minute with 87.5% accuracy — a very small sample that tells us he was cautious and selective on the feet but couldn't solve the grappling problem.
- At 6'4" and 185 pounds, Johnston has legitimate size for the middleweight division, but his reach (76 inches) is actually an inch shorter than Schultz's despite the height advantage — an unusual combination that may limit how effectively he controls distance.
- The age factor matters here. Johnston is 35, which is the acknowledged performance cliff for male middleweights and lighter fighters. Whatever tools he's bringing into this fight, they are unlikely to be improving.
- With a 5-2 career record and no pre-existing UFC data beyond this one fight, the honest assessment is that his skill profile is largely opaque. What we know is that he couldn't stop the submission in the original meeting — and that's the most relevant single data point available.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a rematch with a pre-existing result already on the books: Schultz submitted Johnston in Round 3 of their first meeting. That result shapes everything here. The central question isn't whether Schultz can beat Johnston — he already did — it's whether Johnston has made meaningful adjustments that can alter the structural outcome, and there's no evidence from publicly available data that he has.

Schultz controls this fight through wrestling and submission hunting. Johnston's takedown defense in the UFC is a blank slate statistically, and in the first fight he was taken down and finished — which means his grappling answer to Schultz's game remains unproven. On the feet, Johnston was accurate but low-volume in their first meeting, which is actually a reasonable way to approach a grappler: don't overcommit, don't give up the clinch. The problem is that Schultz's path to the ground doesn't require Johnston to give him openings — he's demonstrated he can get the fight there regardless. Schultz's own striking defense is poor (45%), so Johnston does have a real shot at catching him with something clean if the fight stays standing, but turning a grappler into a standing exchange is easier said than done, especially when the grappler already knows he can make the takedown.

The meaningful vulnerability for Schultz remains his chin. Two KO losses in four fights, both early, is not a sample to dismiss. If Johnston has added genuine striking power or a specific sequence designed to exploit Schultz's tendency to walk into shots, there's a path to an upset. But nothing in the available data suggests Johnston is a heavy-handed finisher, and the first fight didn't produce any knockdown or near-finish from his strikes. The age gap (29 vs. 35) also runs in Schultz's favor over three rounds. The intangible delta here leans clearly to Schultz — younger, with a proven submission game and an existing win over this exact opponent.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Jonathan Micallef -- Style & Profile

- Micallef is a grappling-first fighter who wins by grinding opponents into bad positions rather than by threatening big shots. His four UFC wins include two submissions and two decisions, and his striking output has actually *declined* in each of his last three fights — from 1.67 strikes per minute against Jousset down to 0.4 against Gorimbo in their first meeting. He is not trying to hurt you standing up.
- His submission game is the genuine weapon. Two submission finishes in four UFC fights, paired with 1.1 submission attempts per 15 minutes on career averages, and his grappling accuracy (66%) is respectable. When he gets someone to the mat, he knows what he's doing there.
- His takedown defense (75%) is solid and matters here because Gorimbo is a high-volume wrestler who will absolutely try to negate Micallef's sub game by imposing his own grappling first.
- The striking numbers are a liability. He absorbs 4.22 significant strikes per minute against 3.96 landed — a near-even exchange rate that only holds because he doesn't walk into clean power shots. His strike defense at 56% is below average, and his accuracy at 41% isn't threatening enough to keep opponents honest.
- As a southpaw facing an orthodox opponent, he carries a structural angle advantage that the fight history confirms he uses — he doesn't try to out-box anyone, but the stance difference creates awkward entries that benefit his clinch and grappling setup.
- Already beat Gorimbo once. The win was narrow on the cards (split decision), but he walked out of it with his unbeaten UFC record intact. He has first-hand knowledge of how Gorimbo moves and what his tendencies are, which is not nothing going into a rematch.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Themba Gorimbo -- Style & Profile

- Gorimbo's career numbers paint a picture that his last three fights have completely contradicted. He averages 5.07 takedowns per 15 minutes over his UFC career and 60% striking accuracy — but against Wells, Luque, and Micallef, he threw almost nothing and attempted almost no takedowns. Something broke in his performance level after the Vicente Luque submission, and it hasn't come back.
- The Luque loss is the most important data point in this file. He was finished 52 seconds into Round 1 by submission — meaning he either shot in carelessly or was dragged into a scramble he couldn't escape. For a self-styled wrestler, getting submitted by a striker that quickly is a red flag about his defensive awareness on the ground.
- In his last three fights combined, his SLpM reads 0.73, 0.27, and 5.77 — the last number coming against Luque in that brief opening exchange before the submission. Strip out the Luque fight and Gorimbo has been nearly invisible offensively. The high career striking accuracy (60%) looks like a small-sample artifact from fights against weaker opposition.
- At 35, the age curve is actively working against him. He's past the point where the body bounces back easily from losses, and three straight defeats — including two decisions where he did almost nothing — suggest a fighter whose confidence and execution are both degraded.
- His takedown defense sits at 72%, which is functional but not elite. Against a fighter with submission intentions like Micallef, simply completing a takedown doesn't make Gorimbo safe — it potentially walks him into the submission threat he already demonstrated vulnerability to.
- He's fighting at home (Perth, Australia is familiar ground for many Australian fans, and Gorimbo is a figure popular in the region due to his story), but the crowd advantage doesn't fix a three-fight skid or the tactical problems the first Micallef fight exposed.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

We already have the result of this matchup once — Micallef won a split decision in May 2026 — and the line movement tells us the market now agrees more strongly with that outcome than it did the first time. The structural problem for Gorimbo is the same one Micallef exploited in fight one: Gorimbo's wrestling, which is normally his entire identity, went missing. Zero takedown attempts in the first meeting. His game plan appeared to be to stand and box a southpaw who is awkward to hit, and he barely threw anything. When Gorimbo doesn't wrestle, he has no path to winning.

Micallef's submission threat is the wrinkle that makes Gorimbo's usual approach dangerous. Even if Gorimbo decides to wrestle this time and impose top control, Micallef has the sub game to threaten from the bottom. The first meeting suggests Gorimbo knows this and is uncomfortable with that exchange — his reluctance to shoot may be precisely because he's aware of Micallef's finishing ability on the mat. That's a chess problem Gorimbo hasn't solved yet, and there's no evidence in his camp or his recent performances that anything has changed. The submission loss to Luque — a striker, not a grappler — suggests his ground defensive awareness is actually getting worse, not better.

Micallef's own striking numbers are not inspiring, but against an opponent who threw 0.27 significant strikes per minute in his last outing, he doesn't need to be. The net intangible picture also favors Micallef significantly: he's 27, has won four straight, is fighting with growing confidence, and faces a 35-year-old coming off three consecutive losses whose trademark wrestling has been dormant for months. Gorimbo needs to either rediscover the wrestler he was two years ago or find something completely new. Nothing in the data suggests either is coming.
analysis_sections.endings:

**#1 — Jonathan Micallef wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: The first fight ended this exact way, and neither fighter has demonstrated a dramatically different gear since. Micallef controls range with his southpaw stance and grappling threats, Gorimbo remains reluctant to engage at volume, and the fight grinds to a decision that favors the younger, busier fighter. Micallef's last three fights have all gone the distance, reinforcing that he wins by accumulation rather than punctuation.

**#2 — Jonathan Micallef wins by submission, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Gorimbo decides to actually shoot this time — either to change the outcome or because he gets frustrated standing — he walks into Micallef's submission game, which has two UFC finishes behind it. Gorimbo's vulnerability on the mat (submitted by Luque in under a minute) makes this a realistic outcome the moment the fight hits the ground at Micallef's initiation or Gorimbo's desperation.

**#3 — Themba Gorimbo wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Gorimbo reverts to the wrestler he was against Wells (4.0 takedowns per 15 minutes in that fight) and chains takedowns without giving Micallef submission entries, he can control enough time on top to steal rounds. The first split decision shows at least one judge saw it Gorimbo's way. He would need sustained wrestling activity for 15 minutes that he hasn't shown since 2024 to pull this off.

**#4 — Themba Gorimbo wins by KO/TKO**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Micallef absorbs 4.22 significant strikes per minute and his strike defense (56%) leaves him hittable. Gorimbo does have a first-round KO on his record and the experience to land something meaningful if Micallef gets careless. This requires Gorimbo to be more active offensively than anything in his last three fights suggests he will be, but Micallef's defensive numbers keep it from being impossible.
